questions.question: Define Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP) and list its three main components.
keyPoints: CSP is a problem of finding assignment to variables that satisfies all constraints
Three components: Variables (set X of variables)
Domains (set D of possible values for each variable)
Constraints (set C specifying allowed combinations)

questions.question: Explain the Minimum Remaining Values (MRV) heuristic used in backtracking search for CSP.
keyPoints: MRV selects variable with fewest legal values remaining in its domain
Also known as Most Constrained Variable or Fail-First heuristic
Helps reduce branching factor and identify failures early
Used before assigning values to make search more efficient

questions.question: What is Forward Checking in CSP? How does it differ from Arc Consistency (AC-3)?
keyPoints: Forward Checking removes inconsistent values from neighboring variables after each assignment
AC-3 ensures arc consistency by repeatedly checking all arcs until no domain changes
Forward checking is local, AC-3 is more thorough/iterative
Both are forms of constraint propagation reducing search space

questions.question: Explain the N-Queens problem as a CSP. What are the variables, domains, and constraints?
keyPoints: Variables: One queen per row (N variables)
Domain: Column positions {1,2,...,N} for each queen
Constraints: (1) No two queens in same column, (2) No two queens on same diagonal
Binary constraints between every pair of queens

questions.question: Differentiate between complete and incomplete algorithms for solving CSP.
keyPoints: Complete algorithms (backtracking, AC-3) guarantee solution if one exists
Incomplete algorithms (min-conflicts, hill climbing) are faster but may fail
Complete algorithms explore entire search space systematically
Incomplete algorithms use local search and may get stuck in local optima

questions.question: What is the Least Constraining Value (LCV) heuristic? When is it applied?
keyPoints: LCV selects value that rules out minimum choices for neighboring variables
Prefers values leaving more options for unassigned variables
Applied after variable selection (e.g., via MRV)
Helps preserve flexibility for future assignments

questions.question: Describe the Map Coloring problem as a CSP with an example.
keyPoints: Variables: Regions/states to be colored
Domain: Set of available colors (e.g., {Red, Green, Blue, Yellow})
Constraints: Adjacent regions must have different colors
Binary constraints between neighboring regions
Example: Australia map with 4 colors

questions.question: Explain the backtracking algorithm for solving CSP. Discuss how heuristics like MRV, LCV, and constraint propagation improve its efficiency.
keyPoints: Backtracking assigns variables one at a time, backtracks on conflict
MRV selects most constrained variable to reduce branching
LCV chooses least constraining value to preserve flexibility
Forward checking/AC-3 provide constraint propagation
Together these reduce search space dramatically
Time complexity improved from O(d^n) to much less in practice

questions.question: Explain the AC-3 algorithm for arc consistency. How does it help in solving CSPs? Walk through its steps with an example.
keyPoints: AC-3 maintains arc consistency for binary constraints
Initialize queue with all arcs
For each arc (X,Y), remove inconsistent values from X's domain
Value x in X is inconsistent if no y in Y supports it
Repeat until queue empty or domain becomes empty (no solution)
Example: Show map coloring or simple binary constraint
Reduces domains before search, detecting failures early

questions.question: Solve the following Cryptarithmetic problem as CSP: SEND + MORE = MONEY. Show variables, domains, constraints, and the solution.
keyPoints: Variables: S,E,N,D,M,O,R,Y (8 letters)
Domain: {0,1,2,...,9} with constraint all-different
Leading constraints: S≠0, M≠0
Column constraints with carries: D+E=Y+10*C1, etc.
Apply MRV/LCV/backtracking to solve
Solution: SEND=9567, MORE=1085, MONEY=10652
Shows practical CSP application with multiple constraint types

questions.question: Compare and contrast Backtracking Search, Forward Checking, and AC-3 algorithms for CSP. When would you use each?
keyPoints: Backtracking: complete systematic search, simple but can be inefficient
Forward checking: removes inconsistent values after each assignment, early failure detection
AC-3: ensures arc consistency, more thorough propagation than forward checking
Use backtracking for small problems, forward checking for medium
Use AC-3 or MAC for large problems with many constraints
Trade-off: more propagation = more preprocessing but less search
